questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with BMI 32 presents with a 3-week history of daily headache, worse on lying down, and transient visual obscurations. Neurological exam is normal except for bilateral papilloedema. Brain MRI and MRV are normal. Lumbar puncture opening pressure is 30 cm H2O. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension is most likely in an obese young woman with headache, papilloedema, elevated opening pressure, and normal imaging. Migraine does not cause papilloedema; CVST would appear on MRV; tumour on MRI; meningitis shows CSF pleocytosis.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old man with a history of spina bifida and VP shunt presents with 2 days of headache, vomiting, and drowsiness. He is afebrile. Fundoscopy shows papilloedema. CT head shows dilated ventricles and a collapsed shunt tip. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acute hydrocephalus from shunt obstruction presents with headache, vomiting, drowsiness, papilloedema, and dilated ventricles on CT. Shunt infection would have fever; overdrainage causes small ventricles; haemorrhage would show blood.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension presents with severe headache, blurred vision, and vomiting. Blood pressure is 240/140 mmHg. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema and retinal haemorrhages. CT head is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Malignant hypertension with papilloedema and retinal haemorrhages (hypertensive retinopathy) and normal CT is classic for hypertensive encephalopathy. IIH occurs in younger women; CVST requires MRV; SAH has thunderclap headache and blood on CT; glaucoma presents with eye pain.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman, 6 weeks postpartum, presents with a 2-week history of progressive headache and three episodes of focal motor seizures affecting her left hand. She has papilloedema on exam. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Postpartum headache with seizures and papilloedema is highly suggestive of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis. IIH is less likely with seizures; eclampsia requires hypertension and proteinuria; post-dural puncture headache is postural; migraine with aura lacks papilloedema.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with progressive morning headache over 2 months, worse on coughing, and episodes of confusion. On exam, he has mild right-sided weakness and papilloedema. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Progressive morning headache worsened by Valsalva, focal weakness, and papilloedema indicate a space-occupying lesion, most likely a brain tumour. Subdural haematoma is more acute and often with trauma; NPH has gait, incontinence; stroke is acute; Alzheimer's lacks focal signs.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old obese woman presents with a 3-week history of headache and blurred vision. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema. Neurological examination is otherwise normal. CT head is unremarkable. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of raised intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: In suspected idiopathic intracranial hypertension with normal CT, lumbar puncture with measurement of opening pressure is the gold standard to confirm elevated CSF pressure (typically >25 cmH2O). MRI/MRV are used to exclude secondary causes but do not directly measure pressure.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old university student presents with fever, neck stiffness, and photophobia. GCS is 14. Fundoscopy shows no papilloedema. He has no focal neurological signs. Which investigation should be performed before lumbar puncture?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend a CT brain before lumbar puncture in suspected meningitis if there are high-risk features including GCS <15, focal neurology, seizure, immunocompromise, or papilloedema. This patient has GCS 14, which is a relative contraindication to LP without prior imaging to exclude raised ICP.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with rapidly increasing head circumference, a bulging anterior fontanelle, and 'sunset eyes'. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm hydrocephalus as the cause of raised intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: In infants with an open anterior fontanelle, cranial ultrasound is the first-line, radiation-free investigation to assess ventricular size and confirm hydrocephalus. CT and MRI involve radiation or sedation; LP is dangerous if raised ICP is present.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman on the combined oral contraceptive pill presents with a 1-week history of severe headache, blurred vision, and two seizures. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema. CT head is normal. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the underlying cause of her raised intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: The presentation is classic for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST). MR venography is the non-invasive gold standard for diagnosing CVST, which accounts for the raised ICP. LP would show elevated pressure but not the cause; DSA is invasive; CTV is an alternative but MRV is preferred in UK practice.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old motorcyclist is brought to the emergency department after a high-speed collision. GCS is 6 on arrival. CT head shows multiple cerebral contusions and effacement of the basal cisterns. After intubation and resuscitation, which method is the most reliable to confirm and monitor raised intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: The gold standard for ICP measurement is an intraventricular catheter (EVD), which provides accurate pressure readings and allows therapeutic CSF drainage. Intraparenchymal monitors are less reliable over time; lumbar drains are contraindicated in raised ICP with mass effect; TCD is non-invasive but indirect.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old obese woman presents with headache and blurred vision. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema. MRI brain is normal. Lumbar puncture opening pressure is 35 cmH2O. Diagnosis: idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological treatment?
questions.explanation: Acetazolamide reduces CSF production and is first-line medical therapy for IIH. Weight loss is also first-line but question asks for pharmacological. Topiramate is an alternative but not first-line.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a known cerebellar tumour presents with severe headache, vomiting, and a decreased GCS of 13. CT shows acute hydrocephalus with dilatation of lateral ventricles. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for raised intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: Acute hydrocephalus requires immediate CSF diversion via an EVD. Mannitol may be temporising but definitive therapy is drainage.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old male is brought to ED after a road traffic accident. GCS 8, CT shows diffuse cerebral oedema and no mass lesion. Which treatment is CONTRAINDICATED in the management of his raised intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: The CRASH trial showed corticosteroids increase mortality in traumatic brain injury; thus dexamethasone is contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman with known idiopathic intracranial hypertension is planning a pregnancy. She currently takes acetazolamide. Which of the following treatments is CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Topiramate is a known teratogen and should be avoided in pregnancy. Acetazolamide is category C but can be used if benefit outweighs risk.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old HIV-positive man is diagnosed with cryptococcal meningitis. He has a headache, vomiting, and papilloedema. Lumbar puncture opening pressure is 40 cmH2O. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his raised intracranial pressure?
questions.explanation: In cryptococcal meningitis, raised ICP is managed by repeated therapeutic lumbar punctures to reduce pressure; it improves outcomes.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old obese woman presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of daily headache, worse on lying flat, and blurred vision in both eyes. On examination, she has bilateral papilloedema. Neurological examination is otherwise normal. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement
CT scan of the head without contrast
MRI scan of the head with venography
Refer for optic nerve sheath fenestration
questions.explanation: In suspected idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), imaging to exclude a mass lesion or venous sinus thrombosis is mandatory before lumbar puncture. MRI with venography is the recommended first-line imaging per NICE guidelines to identify sinus thrombosis or structural causes.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with known idiopathic intracranial hypertension currently taking acetazolamide 1 g twice daily and topiramate 50 mg daily presents to the neurology clinic with a 1-week history of worsening headaches and transient visual obscurations. Perimetry shows a new visual field defect in the right eye. She is adherent to her medications. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Add a loop diuretic such as furosemide
Perform repeat lumbar puncture with CSF drainage
Increase acetazolamide to 2 g twice daily
Refer urgently for optic nerve sheath fenestration
questions.explanation: When visual loss progresses despite maximal medical therapy (acetazolamide up to 2g/day and topiramate), surgical intervention such as optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting is indicated. This patient is on adequate medical therapy, and the next step is surgical referral.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a high-speed road traffic accident. He has a GCS of 8 (E1, V2, M5), with bilaterally dilated pupils sluggishly reactive. His heart rate is 50 bpm and blood pressure is 190/100 mmHg. What is the most important immediate next step in management?
options: Elevate head of bed to 30 degrees
questions.explanation: In severe traumatic brain injury with GCS ≤8 and signs of raised intracranial pressure (Cushing's reflex), immediate airway protection via intubation is the priority, followed by controlled ventilation to reduce ICP. This precedes other measures such as mannitol or CT scanning.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known glioblastoma multiforme on dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of worsening headache, nausea, and increasing drowsiness. Her GCS is 13. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Administer a stat dose of intravenous dexamethasone (10-16 mg)
questions.explanation: In a patient with a brain tumour and signs of raised ICP, the immediate step is to give a high dose of dexamethasone to reduce vasogenic oedema. This should be done before imaging if clinical suspicion is high, as it can rapidly improve symptoms. The current low dose is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old university student presents to the emergency department with a 12-hour history of fever, severe headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness. On examination, she has a petechial rash on her trunk and bilateral papilloedema. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Insert an arterial line for blood pressure monitoring
questions.explanation: In suspected bacterial meningitis with papilloedema suggesting raised ICP, lumbar puncture is contraindicated. Antibiotics should not be delayed; give IV ceftriaxone and dexamethasone immediately. The petechial rash is typical of meningococcal disease, so aciclovir is not indicated.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of daily headaches and transient visual obscurations. On examination, visual acuity is 6/6 bilaterally, but fundoscopy reveals bilateral optic disc swelling. Which single finding would necessitate urgent same-day referral to the ophthalmology team?
questions.explanation: A new visual field defect indicates optic nerve damage and is a red flag for progressive vision loss in raised intracranial pressure. Although an enlarged blind spot is common in papilloedema, it does not signify acute damage; a field defect (e.g., concentric constriction) demands urgent intervention.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with known idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) on acetazolamide 500 mg twice daily attends clinic with ongoing headache. Her visual acuities are 6/6 right, 6/9 left. Fundoscopy shows bilateral papilloedema (Frisen grade 2). Which examination finding would be most concerning for progressive vision loss?
questions.explanation: Disc elevation >3 dioptres corresponds to severe papilloedema (Frisen grade 4–5) and is associated with a high risk of permanent visual loss. Mildly reduced visual acuity (6/9) alone is not an emergency threshold; disc elevation is the red flag.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation is brought to the emergency department after a fall. He has a reduced GCS of 9 (E2 V3 M4), and a CT head shows an acute subdural haematoma. During transfer to intensive care, his nurses report that his vital signs show hypertension (180/100 mmHg) and bradycardia (50 beats per minute). Which physical examination finding would now be most concerning for impending brain herniation?
questions.explanation: A unilateral fixed and dilated pupil indicates ipsilateral third nerve palsy due to uncal herniation, an imminent emergency. Bilateral miosis suggests pontine damage; decorticate posturing is a sign of upper brainstem dysfunction but the pupil sign is more specific for herniation.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a known left frontoparietal glioblastoma multiforme on dexamethasone 8 mg twice daily presents with a 2-day history of worsening headache and vomiting. He is now confused. On examination, you note a new left-sided hemiparesis. Which finding is most indicative of a can't-miss complication?
questions.explanation: A new focal neurological deficit raises concern for tumour haemorrhage, significant perilesional oedema, or herniation, requiring urgent imaging. Bilateral papilloedema indicates chronic raised ICP but not an acute deterioration; fever and neck stiffness suggest meningitis, which is less likely given the history.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a known non-functioning pituitary macroadenoma on no regular medications presents with acute onset of severe headache, vomiting, and drooping of his right eyelid. His wife says he has become confused. Which eye finding is most concerning for a life-threatening complication?
options: Right eye with a fixed and dilated pupil and down-and-out deviation
questions.explanation: A fixed and dilated pupil with down-and-out deviation is a complete third nerve palsy, indicative of pituitary apoplexy with lateral extension into the cavernous sinus. This is a neurosurgical emergency. Bilateral ptosis is not typical; homonymous hemianopia is more consistent with occipital lobe pathology.
